question: What is the main advantage of the Result<T, E> type over throw?

options.text: The error becomes part of the function signature and is visible in types
Result is faster than throw
options.explanation: Result is not about performance — its advantage is type safety

options.text: Result automatically logs errors
options.explanation: Result is a data type, it doesn't perform any side effects

options.text: throw cannot be used in TypeScript
options.explanation: throw is fully supported in TypeScript

question: What does the assertNever function do?

options.text: Guarantees at compile time that all union type variants are handled
Prevents the use of null values
options.explanation: assertNever works with the never type in switch/if, not with null

options.text: Checks that a variable is not undefined
options.explanation: assertNever is not related to undefined — it checks exhaustiveness of union types

options.text: Throws an error on every call
options.explanation: assertNever throws at runtime, but the main purpose is a compile error when a variant is missing

question: What happens if you add a new variant to a union type but forget to handle it in a switch with assertNever?

options.text: TypeScript shows a compilation error in the default branch
The code compiles but crashes at runtime
options.explanation: That's the whole point of assertNever — the error occurs at compile time, before the code runs

options.text: The new variant is automatically handled
options.explanation: TypeScript doesn't handle variants automatically — you need to explicitly add a case

options.text: Nothing happens, the code works as before
options.explanation: Without handling the new variant, assertNever receives a non-never type, and compilation fails

question: Which fields are available in AsyncState<T> when status === 'success'?

options.text: status and data
status, data, and error
options.explanation: When status === 'success', the error field doesn't exist — it's a separate union variant

options.text: Only data
options.explanation: The status field is always available — it's the discriminant of the union type

options.text: status, data, error, and loading
options.explanation: A discriminated union provides only the fields of the specific variant, not all at once

question: Why is returning any or null instead of Result an anti-pattern?

options.text: The compiler doesn't force you to check the result
The calling code doesn't know the function can return an error value
The error only manifests at runtime (e.g., null.property)
any and null are forbidden in TypeScript
options.explanation: They're not forbidden, but using them loses the benefits of typing
